I think that "buffer" is the margin of remaining context that if you impinge upon, it triggers the auto-compaction mechanism. This is a soft threshold that precedes the hard threshold of 1M tokens. I was suggesting that if you have autocompact turned on, they should lower the hard threshold.

reply_context.text:
They do this. See autocompact buffer.

However, it's not just the size of the compact prompt, the buffer also needs to have enough space the compaction output, which is probably the bigger chunk. So making the prompt a single token wouldn't help that much.

posts.text: My understanding of the physiology at play was that the CO2 only detaches from the heme group if there's another molecule that wants to bind more strongly, e.g. O2. Per WP, turns out CO2 doesn't bind to heme so doesn't even compete with O2. It's carried by amine groups on the Hgb molecule. TIL.

reply_context.text: citation needed. CO2 binds to hemoglobin. N2 does not. If N2 doesn't bind. there's nothing to displace the bound CO2 and it stays bound, accumulating in the blood. posts.text: citation needed. CO2 binds to hemoglobin. N2 does not. If N2 doesn't bind. there's nothing to displace the bound CO2 and it stays bound, accumulating in the blood. well, if you're not even going to read it, maybe you shouldn't be so sure about what Searle was or was not trying to communicate with the thought experiment.

you started this with a claim about how the essay is normally interpreted. weird to propose interpretations of something you haven't read.

I'm just pointing out that he clearly doesn't reject the systems argument "definitionally" since he spends a sizable fraction of the essay anticipating that argument and dealing with. However you feel about that passage: it's like 10% of the essay. You've already read that much: go read the rest.
fr, it's a short essay. just go read it now. you make a lot of assumptions about what Searle's does and does not do that are addressed directly in the essay. just go read it now.

reply_context.text: Weird that he goes out of his way to address it directly and at length then...

posts.text: Weird that he goes out of his way to address it directly and at length then...

He literally says the book is a stand-in for any arbitrary Turing machine, so no, this isn't too literal. It's just an illustration of why he uses a book here, and how a book manifests a turing machine. The book is a Turing tape. the pages are cells containing instructions and goto statements.

> That's not in dispute

it literally is. Searle doesn't take this for granted and would disagree with my systems position which you are here claiming we should take for granted as fact. Searle absolutely does not.

again: this isn't a question you are posing to the book though: you are posing it to the room. the book doesn't even need to be openable: imagine a book disassembled with each page glued to the wall. the "current page" isn't book state, it's runtime state. it's the position of the person in the room

It certainly is holding state in the book, but the theory of mind is a property of the system. The book doesn't even know what page it's open to. It being open to a given page is a state of the system as a whole. The system carries the TM, not the book alone. QED.

My position is that the semantics are a property of the structure of the dataset, but this is a red herring. It does not matter who wrote the book. All that matters is that book is capable of existing, which is clearly the case because machine translation and instruction following LLMs exist.

reply_context.text: The book is a computer program. Our book was written by SGD. The person in the room is the mechanical operation of the GPU converting an input to an output by applying the instruction parameterized by the model, i.e. the book.

posts.text: The book is a computer program. Our book was written by SGD. The person in the room is the mechanical operation of the GPU converting an input to an output by applying the instruction parameterized by the model, i.e. the book.

reply_context.text: This isn't a nasty question, it's literally the analogy Searle was trying to present. The chinese room is an analogy for a program running inside a computer. Sitting at the interface, you can't differentiate between sending text to an LLM and sending text to a person in a Chinese Room.

reply_context.text: The instructions (book) alone are not sufficient to manifest an entity capable of communication. We need compute (person) and an interface (message passing slot in wall). That entire system (room) is the entity with which we are capable of having an exchange. Not just the book.

reply_context.text: So take the CD over to my friends house where his controller isn't broken: it's a fundamentally different game from the one I was playing at my house. The game is not the CD. The game is the CD + whatever the available runtime is through which it interacts with the world.

posts.text: So take the CD over to my friends house where his controller isn't broken: it's a fundamentally different game from the one I was playing at my house. The game is not the CD. The game is the CD + whatever the available runtime is through which it interacts with the world.

reply_context.text: Consider pre-download-all-the-things gaming. I have a game on a CD, but it's useless until I put it in my playstation. I can only interact with the game as cohesive system:  playstation+cd+TV+controller. The "game" is the whole. E.g. if my joystick is broken, that fundamentally changes the game.

reply_context.text: I think it makes sense to think about "the room" as a unit because the book is a static instruction set. It contains knowledge, but it can't DO anything without a runtime. Communication requires work (Landauer). You need both the runtime and the instruction set together. True for wetware as well.
